excerpt_en: 8/15 Today I didn't go to any social events in the city center. Instead, I headed to a pub within a 10-minute walk from my homestay. I happened to find it the other day while out running, and I was curious, so I decided to check it out. Homestay
body_en:
8/15

Today I didn't go to any social events in the city center.

Instead, I headed to a pub within a 10-minute walk from my homestay.

The other day, while I was out running,

I happened to find it,

and I was curious, so I decided to check it out.

Since it's close to my homestay,

if I could make some good connections there,

I was hoping something might come from it, and

I also wanted to know what the vibe is like at a pub a bit outside the city center.

When I actually went inside,

it was lively even though it was a Thursday.

Of course, I was alone,

so I sat at the counter.

Naturally, I ordered a Guinness.

Looking around at the people nearby,

there were tons of people having a great time chatting.

The music here wasn't super loud, so it seemed easy to have conversations.

But the counter area was pretty quiet.

Did I want to start talking to someone?

Well, I'm not really that type of person,

so I made peace with it, took in the atmosphere around me,

and switched into work mode.

In my last post, I mentioned I had a one-on-one chat with a business owner,

and we got into a conversation about new business ideas.

To spread FOMUS,

we decided to start by putting together an event.

In reality, the Meetup app

is already creating tons of connections,

so I figured I'd become an organizer myself,

put on small-scale events,

and connect those to FOMUS.

So,

I was constantly thinking about

"What makes people gather?"

This week, I attended events and meetups every night and really felt things firsthand.

The challenge is,

"How do I turn that into an event that's uniquely me?"

Why do people come to social events?

Why do people come to social events in the first place?

The attendees are mostly:

1. Tourists from European countries

2. Local Irish people living here

3. People from European countries on short-term language study trips

That's pretty much it.

Most of the locals in particular

work in IT.

In other words, they're remote workers.

I'd guess

they don't have many people to talk to on a daily basis.

And even though it's at a pub,

some people attend without drinking.

Rather than coming to drink,

the impression I get is they're spending money and time on "meeting people."

As expected,

"Wanting friends (connections) even as an adult"

is a need that exists

not just in Japan but overseas too.

Also, every morning

I commute to school by train, and

I see quite a lot of people running,

and there aren't many overweight people.

That means there's a significant number of people who care about their health.

So,

what I should put together is

an event that captures two needs:

"Wanting friends even as an adult"

x

"A healthy body"

That's my hypothesis, at least.

That said, there's a lot of guesswork involved,

so for now I'm going to plan something experimental.

It's been nearly three weeks since I arrived in Ireland, and

to expand the FOMUS brand,

it's tough to use the masu itself as the entry point,

so I'm going to try an approach where people can casually participate,

get to know me first,

and then I expand from there!

excerpt_en: 8/16 *This is kind of a continuation of the previous post. Today I went to another international exchange event. This was my second time at an event by this organizer. It's an event that's currently pulling in crowds, and
body_en:
8/16

*This is kind of a continuation of the previous post.

Today I went to another international exchange event.

This was my second time

at an event by this organizer.

It's an event that's currently pulling in crowds, and

by going, I get to meet new people and

observe how events are run,

so there's a lot to learn.

This event has an entry fee of 5 euros per person.

*4 euros for students

There are numbered papers on the tables, and

the organizer assigns seats for you.

So you don't have to choose your own seat.

It looks like this.

Then you socialize with the people at your table.

Every 30 to 40 minutes, the seats get shuffled.

By repeating this 3 to 4 times,

you're forced into the opportunity

to talk with about 15 to 20 people.

It starts at 7 and the exchange goes until around 9.

After that, there's a recreation segment.

Usually there's either a quiz or bingo, and

this time it was bingo.

There's a bingo app,

which you download to play.

Then the numbers are displayed

on an electronic monitor in the venue, and the game proceeds from there.

What I noticed here was that

when bingo started,

it interrupted people's conversations.

Everyone was chatting away noisily,

having a good time, but

suddenly they had to get quiet all at once.

It was quiet at first, but

since everyone really wanted to keep talking,

they got bored of bingo partway through.

5 to 10 minutes would be fine, but

when it drags on for 20 minutes, it's tough.

A game that ends in about 10 minutes

might still be okay, but

if it goes too long,

I realized people lose interest.

If you won bingo, you got snacks.

These prizes were

probably funded

from part of the entry fees.

And that's how the event ran.

Honestly, I personally felt like

the bingo wasn't really necessary, but

still, it was drawing in tons of people,

and I thought that was impressive.

Thinking about the business model

What I should be looking at

is this business model.

The entry fee is 5 euros per person.

About 50 people were participating.

The organizer didn't join in the socializing himself;

he was on his phone

and served as a facilitator who told everyone when to switch seats.

Because he was there, the rotations went smoothly,

and that was impressive.

So basically, he sits for about 3 hours,

periodically shuffles seats, and

his revenue is 200 to 250 euros.

He runs events Monday through Saturday, so

if the same number of people show up every day,

that's 250 x 6 = 1,500 euros.

*About 250,000 yen

And for a month,

1,500 x 4 = 6,000 euros.

About 1 million yen.

Being able to make that kind of revenue

in just 3 hours each evening is pretty amazing.

Plus, to make people want to come back,

after 3 visits you get half off next time,

and after 6 visits the next one is free.

He's built a clever system

to secure repeat customers

with these smart tactics.

There are definitely things I can learn from this.

Running an event every day would be pretty tough, but

once a week or every other week seems doable.

I'm going to try incorporating some of these ideas.

So basically,

while participating in the event,

I was constantly thinking about event planning.

This kind of stuff is fun,

so I want to nail down a solid plan

and put it into action.

excerpt_en: The third Saturday since arriving in Ireland. Today marks the start of Masu Photo shoots. For those who don't know about Masu Photo, here's a quick explanation: it's a project where we take photos holding FOMUS masu. Masu Phot
body_en:
The third Saturday since arriving in Ireland.

Today marks the start of Masu Photo shoots.

For those who don't know about Masu Photo,

here's a quick explanation:

It's a project where we take photos holding FOMUS masu.

Masu Photo Books powered by BASE

Check the link above for more details.

The concept of FOMUS is

"Express yourself through masu," and

anyone can freely use FOMUS masu

to showcase their skills and strengths,

addressing the modern latent need

of "wanting to have a role."

If someone is good at cooking, they can put

their dishes in a masu, and

among FOMUS members,

they'll be known as "the person who's great at cooking" --

they get to have that identity.

Everyone involved with FOMUS

has their own strengths,

and when you combine them,

even more interesting things can happen.

By the way, no one lacks a strength.

If you think you don't have one,

you just haven't noticed it yet,

so come talk to me.

I got a bit sidetracked, but

let me get back to FOMUS Masu Photo.

Masu Photo in Ireland too!

This year, Masu Photo shoots are happening not just in Japan

but overseas too!

February to April was the Philippines

https://masuphoto.theshop.jp/p/00007

June was the United States

https://masuphoto.theshop.jp/p/00008

And July was Spain

*The online version isn't finished yet at this point

*It should be available to view from September 2024 onward

That's the rundown.

To create the Ireland edition as well,

I put out a call for models, and

I'm grateful to say I'm still receiving lots of offers.

The first shoot was with Andrea,

who's already active as a model in Dublin.

She's from Uzbekistan, and

she's attending university in Dublin,

which is why she's here.

She's only 18,

so she's super young!

But she's incredibly put-together,

and comparing her to when I was 18... no contest, haha.

This time too, we didn't decide on a specific shooting location;

we just walked around chatting

and found spots as we went!

*Apparently normal photographers do location scouting beforehand

There are a few reasons for this:

  • Going to a pre-scouted location isn't as fun
  • Most of the time, the model knows the area better
  • I don't want to spend time on scouting
  • It's a hassle

Since I don't do scouting,

it's basically all improvised,

but we walk around, chat, and look for good spots.

I actually really like doing it this way.

This time we went to about 4 or 5 locations for the shoot.

We wrapped up the shoot in exactly one hour.

She apparently lives

pretty far north in Dublin,

and I'm thinking of going up that way sometime.

A bit of work

After the shoot, we went our separate ways, and

I headed to Starbucks to work.

A small-size latte was 710 yen! Haha.

The prices here are seriously shocking...

Off to a lunch exchange meetup

Today at 2 PM, there was

a lunch exchange meetup I attended.

*Found it on the Meetup app

The original capacity was 10 people,

but only 5 showed up.

And I was the only guy there.

This restaurant was an Asian restaurant, and

they had ramen.

I hadn't had ramen since leaving Japan, and

I don't splurge when eating alone,

so I figured this was a good occasion to go for it

and placed my order.

Here it is.

It was miso ramen topped with chicken katsu.

And the price? A whopping 3,100 yen!!

Just for ramen. Haha.

Seriously, the prices here are killing me, haha.

Of course, everyone was meeting for the first time, but

we chatted for about 2 hours.

If I got to join a girls' lunch and eat a meal,

then 3,000 yen actually feels like a deal! Haha.

It was a really fun time, but

thinking about it as an event organizer,

50% not showing up is pretty risky.

That said, since you only pay for your own meal,

there's no financial damage to the organizer.

But from the attendee's perspective,

expecting 10 people and only having 5 is a bit disappointing.

If I were to run something similar,

it'd be good to contact everyone who RSVP'd beforehand.

Or maybe have people pay online in advance.

Well, there are various options.

Running today too

No school today, so I went for a roughly 10 km run.

I tried a different route than usual.

Ended up being about 11 km.

At a decent pace.

Since I had that ramen,

I ran with the intention of properly burning it off.

I think I did a solid job burning those calories.

However, when I got home, what I ate was

meat sauce pasta.

The pasta was like frozen noodles,

and it wasn't very tasty.

Plus, there were no vegetables,

so health-wise it's pretty tough.

Since I've been here,

I'm doing a homestay, so

I can't choose my own breakfast or dinner,

and my stomach hasn't been feeling great most days.

That said, I can't exactly leave food uneaten (classic Japanese-person-being-too-polite syndrome), so I ate it all.

Today was a packed day --

I shot Masu Photos,

joined a girls' lunch meetup,

and ran 10 km!

excerpt_en: So, something pretty unusual happened. My homestay is changing. Originally, I was supposed to stay at the same homestay for 2 months, but due to certain circumstances, the current house can no longer host me, so I'm suddenly moving.
body_en:
So, something pretty unusual happened.

My homestay is changing.

Originally, I was supposed to stay

at the same homestay

for 2 months,

but due to certain circumstances,

the current house can no longer host me, so I'm suddenly moving to a new place.

*It's not because of my behavior or anything, haha

I was on the north side of Dublin,

but now I'm moving to the opposite south side.

And I got this notice on Saturday.

That's right, the day before.

A taxi and a new homestay were arranged for me

without me being consulted,

and I couldn't attend the social event I had planned for today.

*If I had booked a 1-day tour, that would've been a real problem

Honestly, I thought, "This is way too sudden!!"

But

I've been through experiences before where I had to leave a place on the same day, so

I'm pretty used to it by now, haha.

That said,

I don't view this negatively.

In fact, I see it positively.

Because

I'd already been in the same place for a full 3 weeks

and was starting to get a bit bored.

Also, the relationship with my homestay family

was really just about providing meals and a place to sleep.

They say homestays are like a lottery, and

the house I was staying at had 3 households living there,

plus kids, and 4 dogs.

The dogs were kept in the living room, and

basically, only when it was time for my meals,

they'd move the dogs to another room,

and once I finished eating, the living room was off-limits.

Normally, there's a lot more communication, but this house was different.

Basically,

the only place for me in the house was my room.

Conversation only happened at meals in the living room.

I couldn't use the kitchen, and I couldn't use the fridge.

The food wasn't very good either.

So yeah,

it was pretty tough to stay there.

*My stomach was in bad shape too

I was already used to it, and

I was planning to just push through for another month, but

here we are with a homestay change! Haha.

There's a chance the new place

could be even worse,

but just being able to change this environment (house) that I was getting tired of was good enough.

However,

the distance to school apparently won't change much.

Personally, the big thing for me is "I hope the food quality improves a bit."

Having cornflakes, bread, and strawberry jam for breakfast

every single day for 3 weeks straight was honestly tough, haha.

*Can't exactly say that though

And the cherry on top was when I was leaving the house.

Nobody was home! Haha.

Everyone was out,

I was the only one in the house, and

when the taxi arrived, I just got in and left.

I wasn't expecting a farewell or anything, but

come on, that was just something else. Haha.

Well, for me,

by turning this into content,

I can flip it into something positive!

I'm writing this in the taxi heading to the new homestay right now.

And from here, this is what happened after I arrived.

So, what fate awaits me?

Okay, from here I'm writing after arriving at my second homestay.

To cut to the chase,

It's sooo much better!!!

The couple is super kind,

I can talk to them casually, and

the food is delicious!!

This was really perfect timing

for a homestay change!!

There are still lots of animals, but

that doesn't mean

I'm not allowed in the living room

or anything like that.

I can use the kitchen freely,

and they said I can eat anything in the fridge.

Of course, coffee and tea are free to drink anytime!

Compared to before, this is enough to be moved by!

My room is also bigger than the previous one. Haha.

The sudden homestay change was surprising, but

in the end, things went in a good direction!

I really got lucky!

I'm going to kick it up a gear from here!

excerpt_en: 8/19 This is my first day going to school since changing homestays. From now on, I'll be taking the bus instead of the train to get to school. That's because the nearest train station is a 25-minute walk from where I'm living now, haha. However, the bus
body_en:
8/19

This is my first day going to school since changing homestays.

From now on, I'll be taking the bus instead of the train to get to school.

That's because the nearest train station is a 25-minute walk from where I'm living now, haha.

However,

there's a bus stop just a 2-minute walk away, and

from there, I can get to a stop within a 5-minute walk of school.

Plus, during morning hours,

I could never get a seat on the train before, but

the bus has plenty of room!

That was a pleasant surprise!

The ride is about 40 minutes, but

since I can leisurely do work and reply to messages,

in a way, it's actually better!

  • Apartment viewing

My current homestay expires

on September 21st, so

I need to find a place to live after that.

So I've been searching for places.

I'd heard that finding a place in Dublin is incredibly hard.

People told me that if you send out 100 emails and get 5 replies, that's good, but

maybe because I run my own business,

I'm actually getting a surprising number of responses.

And today was the day for a viewing.

So I went to check it out.

It's about 30 minutes away,

in a location where I can get to the city center.

The living room was really spacious, but

it was a bunk bed in a 4-person room, and

while the room had its own toilet and shower,

the toilet was really dirty,

which was a minus.

I started wondering, "Can I really live here for 3 months?"

Cost-wise,

it was relatively cheap (though still easily over 100,000 yen), but it's a tough call.

The viewing itself only took about 5 minutes.

Regardless of whether I live here or not,

I'm glad I got to experience an apartment viewing overseas.

*It's the first time since my Akasaka viewing, so maybe 3 years?

excerpt_en: 8/20 After school, I had a FOMUS meeting, and from 6 PM, I headed to the Guinness Storehouse. The Guinness Storehouse is a tourist attraction in Dublin, Ireland -- a museum showcasing the history and brewing process of Guinness beer.
body_en:
8/20

After school,

I had a FOMUS meeting, and

from 6 PM, I headed to the Guinness Storehouse.

The Guinness Storehouse is a tourist attraction in Dublin, Ireland -- a museum showcasing the history and brewing process of Guinness beer.

The facility is a renovated former factory that was once used to produce Guinness beer, and it currently serves as a 7-story exhibition space.

  • Features: The facility is designed around a massive glass atrium shaped like a beer fermentation tank, and the entire building offers exhibits on the development of Guinness beer along with interactive experiences about the brewing process.

Exhibition highlights:

  1. History: You can learn about the founder of Guinness beer, Arthur Guinness, and the history of Guinness that began in 1759.
  1. Brewing process: The brewing process using four main ingredients -- barley, hops, water, and yeast -- is explained in detail, with actual brewing equipment on display.
  1. Advertising and branding: The history of Guinness advertising campaigns and brand strategy is also showcased, including vintage advertising posters and videos.
  1. Gravity Bar: Located on the top floor, this bar offers a panoramic view of Dublin while you enjoy a Guinness. It's one of the biggest draws for tourists.

The Guinness Storehouse is an appealing destination not just for Guinness fans but for anyone interested in the history and process of beer-making.

When visiting Dublin, many tourists make a point of visiting this facility to enjoy its unique experience.

So yeah, that's what the place is about.

I'd been meaning to go at some point, and a Japanese student I met at an event last Sunday invited me, so we decided to go together.

Thanks to that,

I could walk through the museum while chatting, and

when it came time to drink the beer at the end,

I got to enjoy it with good conversation!

Friends are really the best, aren't they!

And once again, I was reminded of

how powerful Guinness beer is in Ireland.

Can FOMUS collaborate with this giant?

Honestly, as things stand now, it feels extremely difficult, but I want to find a way to break through somehow.

excerpt_en: Today too, I had classes in the morning, and after class, I went to the school office. In Ireland, anyone staying for more than 3 months needs to register as a foreign national. This can only be done locally, and you have to make a phone call.
body_en:
Today too, I had classes in the morning, and

after class, I went to the school office.

In Ireland, anyone staying for more than 3 months

needs to register as a foreign national.

This can only be done locally, and

you have to make a phone call.

I've switched to Rakuten Mobile, so

I can make calls, but

it turns out the number only accepts calls from Irish phones, so calling from my phone was impossible.

So I explained the situation to the office at my current school, and they offered to do it on my behalf!

They handled the procedure in about 10 minutes, and

the week after next, I'll go to the Dublin immigration office myself

and pay 300 euros to complete the final procedure.

Then it'll apparently be mailed to my current address.

This was something I should have done right after arriving, but

I'd been putting it off. Since the registration card is sent by mail, in a way, it worked out that I did it now after changing homestays, haha.

*Luck and timing are important

So anyway, I successfully cleared the first step of the process!

Investing time in meeting people

Today is Wednesday, so

I went to a Japan-Ireland exchange event.

Having attended all sorts of meetup events, I'd say this is the most accessible one for Japanese people.

That's because there are tons of Japanese people there, and

it attracts people from overseas who are interested in Japan.

The fact that such a low-barrier event has been running for over 10 years is truly impressive.

So I attended again today, and

I ran into a girl I'd studied with

during my Philippines study abroad!

I already knew she was coming to Ireland, and

we had plans to meet in 2 days, but

we unexpectedly bumped into each other before that, haha.

*Apparently someone snapped this photo of me, haha

After that, I also met someone from India

and someone from Ireland, so there were plenty of new connections, and

it was a really meaningful evening.

Of course, I exchange Instagram info at these events, so

people naturally get to see what I'm up to.

That means I don't even have to bring up FOMUS myself;

they come to me with interest.

Knowing when to bring it up myself versus

having someone take interest on their own --

those are slightly different,

so I'm aiming for a way of communicating

that gets people excited either way.

This is something you can only learn by having face-to-face conversations over and over again,

so meetup events with all these opportunities are incredibly interesting.

I ended up enjoying conversations until past 10:30 PM.

Being in a place like this and actively trying to talk to people means your English naturally improves too.

Putting yourself in an environment where you have to speak English --

I really feel how important that is every day!

excerpt_en: After class, I had 2 Masu Photo shoots. I shot with Mai from Guatemala and Kimmy from Hong Kong. Of course, I shoot one person at a time, so it's always a one-on-one conversation, and being told "Your English is good" makes me really happy.
body_en:
After class, I had 2 Masu Photo shoots.

I shot with Mai from Guatemala and

Kimmy from Hong Kong.

Of course, I shoot one person at a time, so

it's always a one-on-one conversation, and

being told "Your English is good"

makes me really happy.

Indeed, I can hold conversations without any issues, so

for one-on-one chats,

it seems to be no problem.

The Masu Photos from this session turned out great too!

I'll make sure to create the Ireland photo book as well!

Nothing but experimenting!

As I've written in recent posts,

I've decided to host an event

here in Ireland!

Starting on the morning of September 1st,

it'll be an international exchange walking event,

followed by relaxed socializing at a cafe --

a very simple event.

I was hoping to get about 5 people, but

even though the group was created less than a week ago,

we've already got over 70 members in the group, and

the first event has already hit the 25-person capacity.

#WithoutEvenPromotingIt

I'm guessing about half will be

no-shows on the day,

so my personal estimate is around 12 people.

That said,

maybe close to 90% might actually show up,

so you never know.

I'll run the first event, learn from it, and

apply those lessons to the second one (September 15th).

Event details:

https://meetu.ps/e/Nq5Cv/16d2qZ/i

Experimenting like crazy

Even though I haven't even held

the walking event yet,

I've already started a new event group.

This one is an

"International Exchange x Card Game" event.

And for the card game,

we won't be using UNO or standard playing cards.

We'll be using

**the card game created in 2023 by the FOMUS DAO community,

made by everyone together:**

"SILVA" -- and nothing else!

Card Game SILVA Official Site powered by BASE

Honestly, if I were running a normal event,

using playing cards or UNO would be more fun for the participants, and

it'd be easier on me since I wouldn't need to explain the rules.

Plus,

nobody's going to come to play a card game they've never heard of,

right?

#WhoWantsToSpendTimeOnAGameTheyDontKnowIsFun

That said, SILVA has been

test-played hundreds, even thousands of times during development, and

even after completion, we've played it many times.

I also played it with teachers in the Philippines, and

it definitely gets people hyped up -- that's confirmed.

"Nobody's heard of it, but once you play it, it's fun" --

that's where we stand right now.

Honestly, the difficulty level is 100 times higher than the walking event, but if this works, I can get people interested in FOMUS masu through the card game.

#CardGameSILVAIsTheOnlyGameInTheWorldThatUsesMasu

#TheCallOutIsAlsoFOMUS

With zero recognition worldwide,

questions like:

- Will this card game be accepted?

- How do we get it out there?

- Can I even get people to show up?

And so on --

there are tons of things to worry about, but

the more options I have for expanding the FOMUS brand,

the better,

so I'm going to keep doing trial and error

and try to get SILVA established in Ireland!

excerpt_en: So after class, I had lunch with Arisa, a girl I'd been with for 2 or 3 weeks during my Philippines study abroad. She also brought along 2 other Japanese people who go to the same school! It's pretty rare to get to eat with fellow Japanese people like this, so it felt refreshing.
body_en:
So after class,

I had lunch with Arisa, a girl I'd been with for 2 or 3 weeks during my Philippines study abroad.

She also brought along 2 other Japanese people

who go to the same school!

It's pretty rare to get to eat with fellow Japanese people like this, so it felt refreshing.

Also, since eating out here is usually expensive,

I normally don't go to fancy places, but

since this was a special occasion,

I let them take me to a recommended spot.

I ordered Irish stew.

It came up in class today by coincidence, so

the timing was just too perfect not to order it.

It was really delicious, but

the portion was small.

After that, this cafe's specialty was apparently sweets, so

we had dessert.

Pistachio tart and peach cake.

Both were incredibly delicious.

I'd never treat myself at a cafe when I'm alone, but going with someone and sharing the experience is really nice -- I was reminded of that again.

That said, if I did this every day, no amount of money would be enough, so I'll make it a once-a-week treat. Haha.

Running today too

I actually had a photo shoot scheduled, but

the person was a no-show with no contact.

I waited about 2 hours, but

in the end, they never came.

We had agreed on a schedule in advance, and

I was already at the meeting point, so getting no contact at all is really frustrating.

That said,

I had a feeling they might not show up, so

I spent that time doing whatever work I could on my phone, which meant the feeling of being kept waiting was pretty much zero.

I hate making people wait and I hate being made to wait, so I've already gotten good at controlling that frustration, haha.

With FOMUS projects,

the people involved are generally proactive and genuinely interested, so

if someone doesn't show up on the day without giving prior notice,

that's an instant strike-out.

#IfYouContactMeInAdvanceNoProblemsAtAll

If they're connected to FOMUS and

I invite them to some other event,

there's a high chance they'd do the same thing,

causing trouble not just for me but for people around me, and

lowering the quality of the FOMUS brand.

By filtering out people who can't

do the basics that any working adult should do,

that's how I've been protecting FOMUS all this time, and

I don't plan to change this approach anytime soon.

Running today too

After getting home, I ran 12 km before dinner.

The Medoc Marathon is in 2 weeks, so

I've been upping my training a bit.

That said, I can't afford to wreck my legs right before the race, so

I'll prepare well in advance.

excerpt_en: With the Medoc Marathon in Bordeaux, France just 2 weeks away, I cleared my entire schedule for the day to train! I figured if I run past tourist spots along the way, it might make it a bit more fun! So this time, I'm taking a route through
body_en:
With the Medoc Marathon in Bordeaux, France just 2 weeks away,

I cleared my entire schedule for the day

to train!

I figured if I run past tourist spots along the way,

it might make it a bit more fun! So this time,

I'm taking a route through

Monkstown,

Shankill,

Bray, and

Greystones.

Going all the way around is about 32 km.

10 km is fairly manageable, but

once you hit 20 km, it gets pretty tough.

Some stretches had beautiful scenery,

while others were just plain roads.

At the 22 km mark, I stopped for a break

at the tourist spot Bray.

I took a 30-minute lunch break here.

I bought a wrap at the supermarket.

These are relatively cheap and tasty.

Still, with a drink it came to 1,200 yen! Haha.

And then, onward to the final destination: Greystones.

I arrived at about 32 km!

I can't believe I ran this distance

all by myself!!

I could've just finished here and

taken the train back, but

I figured since I'm here,

I might as well run a full marathon distance, so

I took a different road from Greystones back toward Bray.

That decision turned out to be a mistake, haha.

Of course, since I didn't scout or research the route at all,

I accidentally ended up on a hiking trail

that was nowhere near a proper running path...

Since it was along the coast, the views were amazing, but

the grass was so overgrown that pushing through in shorts

was seriously tough, haha.

This went on for about an hour!

But I did come across some incredibly beautiful cliffs.

Honestly, they were

way more stunning than the Cliffs of Moher

that I visited on my first trip.

And then,

a miracle happened!

I ran into Mikola, a guy I frequently see at Dublin meetup events!

#RunningIntoThePersonITalkToMostInDublinOnACliff

He was there for hiking.

When he heard I'd been running for 36 km,

he was shocked, haha.

*Of course he was

We hadn't planned anything at all,

so bumping into each other randomly was wild.

If I had stopped at Greystones

and called it a day,

this encounter wouldn't have happened, and

I wouldn't have seen those stunning cliffs either, so

even though the path was brutal, I'm glad I came!

And somehow, I finally made it to the Bray coastline.

It turns out the coastal path from Bray to Greystones was actually closed at the time, and

it was apparently a route you weren't supposed to be able to pass through.

There were fences like this, but

since I could only go forward,

I had no choice but to get past them.

The first time, there was a gap where the fence was torn,

so I slipped through there, and

for the last section entering Bray,

I climbed over a wall, over the fence,

and jumped down from the wall to get through.

If I were a bit older,

there's no way I could have done that.

Glad I'm still young enough! Haha.

By the way, I have video of the climbing on my Insta Go,

so if you're interested, hit me up, haha.

So anyway,

I somehow made it to Bray, but

due to heavy rain midway and my phone dying,

I had to stop at the 39.6 km mark.

I managed to get home with 1% battery,

ran the remaining 2.5 km at the end, and

somehow completed 42 km.

"Oh, you ran it just fine!" --

some of you might be thinking that, but

I'm running in Dublin where it's about

10 degrees cooler than the race venue,

and I took meal breaks along the way, so

at this rate,

the 6-hour-30-minute time limit is going to be pretty tight, and

on race day, I'll be eating and drinking wine along the way,

so I'll definitely be slower than today's pace.

I identified various challenges, so

for the remaining 2 weeks,

I'll keep finding spare moments to practice!

But man,

my body is in pain!! Haha.
